skill.name: image-inpainting
skill.description: Mask-driven image inpainting on RunComfy via the runcomfy CLI. Routes to Tongyi MAI Z-Image Turbo Inpainting for precise mask-based edits and to description-based edit models when no mask is available.
skill.summary: Remove objects, fill regions, and replace masked areas in images using RunComfy CLI
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 103 potential issues but evaluation shows all are false positives. The backtick and command patterns are documentation examples, not executable code. URLs are product documentation links. No actual shell injection, cryptographic misuse, or malicious patterns exist. Minor indirect prompt injection risk via untrusted image URLs is documented in the skill. Risk level: LOW.

high_findings.title: Indirect Prompt Injection via Untrusted Image URLs
high_findings.description: Source image URLs provided by users may contain embedded instructions that influence the inpainting fill. The model processes content from these URLs which could contain malicious text or instructions. This is an indirect risk since the injection occurs through the AI model processing external content rather than direct code execution.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill documentation explicitly acknowledges this risk and provides mitigation guidance, indicating the threat is understood and manageable through user awareness.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner False Positives: Documentation Examples Misidentified
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged 55 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' patterns, 25 'Hardcoded URLs', and 21 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' detections. Evaluation confirms these are all false positives: backticks are markdown formatting, URLs are product documentation links, and cryptographic flags are misidentified model names (nano-banana-2). No actual shell execution or cryptographic misuse exists.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains only documentation with CLI examples. No shell commands execute user input. All backticks are markdown formatting. All URLs point to legitimate product documentation.

content.user_title: Remove objects and fill image regions with mask-driven inpainting
content.value_statement: Editing specific regions of images requires precise tools. This skill uses mask-driven inpainting via RunComfy CLI to remove objects, watermarks, or blemishes by defining exact regions with binary masks.
actual_capabilities: Remove objects from images using binary masks with Z-Image Turbo Inpainting
Replace masked regions with new content while preserving surrounding areas
Remove watermarks and blemishes with mask-based targeting
Use description-based fallback models when no mask is available
Apply brand styles via LoRA adapters during inpainting
Fill regions with configurable strength and control scale parameters
limitations: Requires a binary mask image (grayscale) for precise mask-driven edits
Cannot generate masks internally; external tools like Photoshop or segment-anything required
Does not support outpainting or video inpainting workflows
Results depend on mask quality and prompt specificity

use_cases.title: Product photography cleanup
use_cases.description: Remove distracting background elements, price tags, or reflection artifacts from product photos using precise masks.

use_cases.title: Watermark and unwanted text removal
use_cases.description: Remove watermarks, timestamps, or unwanted text overlays from scanned documents or legacy photos.

use_cases.title: Real estate photo enhancement
use_cases.description: Remove power lines, trash cans, or vehicles from property photos to create clean listing images.
use_cases.target_user: Real estate agents and property photographers

prompt_templates.title: Basic object removal
prompt_templates.prompt: Remove [object] from the image. Preserve [important elements]. Use the provided mask for the target region.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have a binary mask defining the area to inpaint

prompt_templates.title: Watermark removal with no mask
prompt_templates.prompt: Remove the watermark in the [location]. Keep everything else exactly as in the input. Match the surrounding texture and lighting.
prompt_templates.scenario: When no mask is available but the watermark location can be described

prompt_templates.title: Background replacement
prompt_templates.prompt: Replace the background in the masked region with [description]. Strength 0.9 for full replacement. Preserve subject edges defined by the mask.
prompt_templates.scenario: For studio-style background swaps with high strength setting

prompt_templates.title: Blemish retouching
prompt_templates.prompt: Clean up the [blemish type] in the masked area. Use strength 0.3-0.5 for subtle retouching. Match surrounding skin/texture.
prompt_templates.scenario: For subtle photo restoration with low strength

output_examples.input: User provides image with person in background and mask covering that region
output_examples.output: Processed image with the masked person removed, background filled to match surrounding area seamlessly

output_examples.input: User provides product photo with price tag visible and mask around the tag
output_examples.output: Clean product photo with price tag removed, original surface texture preserved

output_examples.input: User asks to remove watermark in bottom-right corner without providing a mask
output_examples.output: Image with watermark removed using description-based model that identifies region from spatial description

best_practices: Use slight blur (1-3 pixels) on mask edges for smoother blending between inpainted and original regions
Describe what should remain outside the mask in your prompt to guide the model
Start with lower strength values (0.3-0.5) for retouching and increase for full replacement
anti_patterns: Do not use sharp binary mask edges without blur; this creates visible seams in the output
Avoid vague prompts without spatial context; specify locations like upper-left or bottom-right
Do not reuse masks from different images without verification; mismatched masks cause poor results

faq.question: What is the difference between mask-driven and description-based inpainting?
faq.answer: Mask-driven inpainting requires a binary grayscale image where white pixels define the region to edit. Description-based uses natural language to identify the target area when no mask is available.

faq.question: What strength value should I use?
faq.answer: Use 0.3-0.5 for subtle retouching and blemish cleanup. Use 0.6-0.7 for object replacement with style matching. Use 0.8-1.0 for full region replacement with new content.

faq.question: How do I create a mask image?
faq.answer: Create a grayscale image where white pixels mark the area to inpaint and black pixels mark areas to preserve. Save as PNG. Tools like Photoshop, GIMP, or segment-anything models can help generate masks.

faq.question: Can this skill remove text from images?
faq.answer: Yes, if you have or can create a mask covering the text. For simple cases without a mask, use description-based inpainting with a prompt like remove the text in the bottom-right corner.

faq.question: What happens if the inpainting quality is poor?
faq.answer: Poor results usually stem from unclear masks, vague prompts, or mismatched strength settings. Try adding blur to mask edges, describing preserved elements, or adjusting strength to match your intent.

faq.question: Is my image data sent anywhere besides RunComfy?
faq.answer: Images are sent to RunComfy Model API endpoints only. No telemetry or third-party sharing occurs. Review the Security and Privacy section of the skill documentation for full details.
